title: A guide to reporting mokopuna progress and achievement - Ministry of Education
content:
## A guide to reporting mokopuna progress and achievement
Find out how reporting for Te Marautanga o Aotearoa will make it easier to see your mokopuna progress and achievement at kura.
- How the report shows what each mokopuna is learning
- Understanding Tohu Aromatawai
- How kaiako will select Tohu Aromatawai
- What I'll see in a mokopuna report
- Supporting mokopuna with complex learning needs
We’re inviting whānau to share their feedback on the Parent Portal to help us improve the information and support we provide. Take a few minutes to complete our short survey.
Parent Portal survey – Survey Monkey
You will notice changes to the reo versions of the Tohu Aromatawai. These changes have been made to align with the changes to Te Marautanga o Aotearoa for consistency.
The revised Tau 0–10 Te Reo Rangatira and Pāngarau wāhanga ako in Te Marautanga o Aotearoa bring changes to how kura and kaiako report on mokopuna progress.
These changes make reporting clearer, more consistent, and more useful for parents and whānau.
Reports now include:
- 1 of 5 Tohu Aromatawai to describe progress
- a narrative explaining why the Tohu Aromatawai was chosen
- ideas for supporting learning at home
- a visual showing progress over time
- aromatawai results help us understand your mokopuna progress, attendance information, recognising its link to learning.
Most kura will start using the new way of reporting mokopuna progress and achievement this year. Kura that are run by a specified kura board can keep using their current curriculum (the one used in 2025), as well as their usual ways of assessing learning, aromatawai, and reporting to whānau, until the end of 2026.
These kura can choose to start using the updated curriculum and new tools during 2026, if they feel ready. However, they do not have to make this change until the beginning of 2027.

### What a specified kura board is #
A specified kura board includes the board of:
- a kura kaupapa Māori
- a designated character school with a hapū or iwi based character, or one that is linked with Ngā Kura ā Iwi o Aotearoa
- a state integrated school with a hapū or iwi based special character
More information on the new Te Marautanga o Aotearoa is available on the Parent Portal page.

## How the report shows what each mokopuna is learning #
This diagram helps show how mokopuna progress is tracked through the year.
Describing progress for mid and end of year reporting using 5 descriptors
The tohu aromatawai describe mokopuna progress across the school year.
- Mid-year reporting: Making progress, although not yet on track to meet curriculum expectations, if e maianga ana | emerging or e whanake haere ana | developing.
- Mid-year reporting: On track to meet curriculum expectations if e ū haere ana | consolidating , e mātau ana | proficient or e kairangi ana | exceeding.
- End of year reporting: Has not fully met curriculum expectations if e maianga ana | emerging, e whanake haere ana | developing or e ū haere ana | consolidating.
- End of year reporting: Has met curriculum expectations if e mātau ana | proficient or e kairangi ana | exceeding.
If at mid-year a kaiako considers mokopuna to be:
- e maianga ana or e whanake haere ana , this means mokopuna are still learning critical knowledge for their year level and needs some support. They are making progress, but they’re not yet on track to meet the end of year expectations.
- e ū haere ana , e mātau ana or e kairangi ana , this means mokopuna are on track for their year level. They are learning well and building confidence.
If at the end of the year, the kaiako decides mokopuna are:
- e mātau ana or e kairangi ana , mokopuna have met or exceeded the expectations for their year level
- e maianga ana , e whanake haere ana , or e ū haere ana , mokopuna have made progress and is learning steadily, but they have not yet met all the expectations for their year level.
For mokopuna with diverse learning needs, or who have individualised progress goals and assessments outlined in their Individual Education Support Plans (IEP), reporting should reflect progress toward those personalised goals, alongside curriculum expectations.

## Understanding Tohu Aromatawai #
Tohu Aromatawai are intended to provide a consistent language to report on progress. They are clear, nuanced, strength based, and help identify where mokopuna are achieving, and what is needed to support further progress.
The goal for the end of the year is that mokopuna reach 'E mātau ana' or above, showing they are ready for the next year of learning.
The table below explains each of the 5 Tohu Aromatawai you may see on a report for your mokopuna.
- E karirangi ana  - Kei tua atu i te taumata i whakaritea rā tā te mokopuna whakatutuki i ngā whāinga marautanga o tana tau kura.
Mō te taha ki tō mokopuna:
- E whakaatu whakaaro whatutoto ana mā tana mārama ki ngā ariā uaua, mā te hīraurau hopanga, me te hanga hononga me te whakamārama i ōna whakaaro i runga i te mārama.
- E taea ana te hono i tāna i ako ai i tētahi wāhi ki tētahi anō.
- E taea ana te whakapakari i ana whakaaro kia puta ai he māramatanga
- E pai ana ia ki ngā wero me ngā arawātea e hōhonu ake ai ōna māramatanga,
- e whakamahi ai hoki i ngā āhua whakaaro e hou ana
- E mātau ana - E tutuki haere ana i te mokopuna ngā whāinga marautanga o tana tau kura
Mō te taha ki tō mokopuna:
- E taea ana te whakamahi ko tōna kotahi anake, ka mutu e mōhio ana ki ngā ara whakamahi e pai ana.
- E taea ana te whakamahi i ōna mōhiotanga i ngā tūāhua rerekē i runga i te tūhake.
- Whakamahia ai tāna i ako ai ki ngā tūāhua hou.
- E taea ana te āta whakapuaki whakaaro me te whakamārama i ōna whakaaro.
- E ū haere ana - E tutuki haere ana i te mokopuna ngā whāinga marautanga maha o tana tau kura, ā, e pakari haere ana ōna māramatanga.
Mō te taha ki tō mokopuna:
- E ako tikanga whaitake ana hei tautoko i ana ako.
- E piki ana tōna māia ki te maumahara me te whakamahi i tāna i ako ai.
- E piki ana tōna māia ki te whakamahi i tāna i ako ai i ngā tūāhua rerekē.
- E taea ana te tiritiri whakaaro, e tīmata ana anō hoki ki te kōrero mō ngā mahi rerekē ka mahia hei whakarei ake i ana mahi.
- E whanake haere ana - E whanake haere ana te mokopuna i roto i ngā whāinga marautanga o tana tau kura.
Mō te taha ki tō mokopuna:
- E tīmata ana tōna māramatanga ki ngā ariā matua, me ētahi tautoko tonu.
- E taea ana te maumahara ki ngā meka me te whakamahi i tāna i ako ai i ngā horopaki kua waia kē, me te paku āwhina.
- Mā ngā tautoko tika, e koke whakamua ana e tutuki ai te kawatau mō tōna taumata tau
- E maiangi ana - Me whai tautoko te mokopuna e tutuki ai ngā whāinga marautanga o tana tau kura, ngā whāinga rānei e whakaahuahia ana i tana mahere ako whaiaro
Mō te taha ki tō mokopuna:
- E tīmata ana tōna māramatanga ki ētahi ariā ako mō tōna taumata tau, me te nui o te tautoko.
- E taea ana i ētahi wā te whakamahi te mōhiotanga nāna i ako, engari ko te tikanga hiahia āwhina ai kia pērā.
E koke whakamua ana mā ngā āhua e whaihua ana, e hāngai ana anō hoki ki ōna kaha, ōna hiahia, me te nui o te tautoko
- E kairangi ana – Exceeding Mokopuna are exceeding curriculum expectations for their year level. Mokopuna: Shows advanced thinking by understanding tricky ideas, solving problems, making connections and explaining their thinking clearly. Can link what they’ve learned in one area to another. Can build on their ideas to show deeper understanding. Enjoys extra challenges and opportunities to deepen their understanding and think in new ways.
- Shows advanced thinking by understanding tricky ideas, solving problems, making connections and explaining their thinking clearly.
- Can link what they’ve learned in one area to another.
- Can build on their ideas to show deeper understanding.
- Enjoys extra challenges and opportunities to deepen their understanding and think in new ways.
- E mātau ana – Proficient Mokopuna are achieving curriculum expectations for their year level. Mokopuna: Can use helpful learning techniques on their own and knows how to use them well. Can independently use their knowledge in different situations. Can often use what they know in new situations. Can share their thoughts clearly and explain their reasoning.
- Can use helpful learning techniques on their own and knows how to use them well.
- Can independently use their knowledge in different situations.
- Can often use what they know in new situations.
- Can share their thoughts clearly and explain their reasoning.
- E ū haera ana – Consolidating Mokopuna are meeting many curriculum expectations for their year level and are steadily strengthening their understanding across whānau ako. Mokopuna: Is learning helpful techniques on their own to support their learning. Is becoming more confident at remembering and using what they have learned. Is becoming more confident to use what they have learned in different situations Can share their ideas and beginning to talk about what they would do differently to enhance their work.
- Is learning helpful techniques on their own to support their learning.
- Is becoming more confident at remembering and using what they have learned.
- Is becoming more confident to use what they have learned in different situations
- Can share their ideas and beginning to talk about what they would do differently to enhance their work.
- E whanake haere ana – Developing Mokopuna are making some progress towards curriculum expectations for their year level. Mokopuna: Is beginning to understand key ideas, often with some support. Can remember facts and use what they have learned in familiar situations, with a bit of help. With the right support, they are making progress towards the expectation for their year level.
- Is beginning to understand key ideas, often with some support.
- Can remember facts and use what they have learned in familiar situations, with a bit of help.
- With the right support, they are making progress towards the expectation for their year level.
- E maiangi ana – Emerging Mokopuna require tailored support to meet curriculum expectations for their year level and/or goals as described in their personalised learning plan. Mokopuna: Is beginning to understand some learning ideas for their year level with a lot of support. Can sometimes use knowledge they’ve learned but usually needs help to do so.
- Is beginning to understand some learning ideas for their year level with a lot of support.
- Can sometimes use knowledge they’ve learned but usually needs help to do so.
Is making some progress in ways that are meaningful and aligned to their strengths and needs, with significant support.

## How kaiako will select Tohu Aromatawai #
Kaiako use a wide range of information to understand how mokopuna are progressing and achieving. This includes what they notice in the akomanga, conversations with mokopuna, and examples of their learning throughout the year.
If you would like to see more detail about the information used, you can ask your mokopuna and their kaiako. Some kura may also include this information in reports, share it during parent and whānau‑kaiako meetings, or make it available through online school portal.
Throughout the year, kaiako collect information from classroom observations, conversations in tairongo, completed work, and assessment tools like SMART or Hihira Weteoro. Putting all this together helps them understand mokopuna progress, achievement and success.
No single piece of evidence gives the full picture. Kaiako bring together everything they know about mokopuna to decide which Tohu Aromatawai best reflects their progress at that time.

## What I'll see in a mokopuna report #
At least twice a year, kura are required to provide a written report to mokopuna, and their parents and whānau, in plain language on mokopuna progress and achievement across the curriculum. From 2026, kura will be expected to include the following 5 components. This will be in addition to other information the kura currently includes in its reports.
- Tohu Aromatawai for each of Te Reo Rangatira and Pāngarau
- information about how Tohu Aromatawai were selected, and about how you can support learning at home.
- a visual representation of mokopuna progress over time
- an attendance record.
These components are not designed to replace current reporting practices, but they need to be included in some way in all reports from the start of 2026 onwards.
Below is an example of how these elements may appear in a report for your mokopuna. Existing report structures can still be used by kura as long as these elements are included.

### A visual showing progress over time #
A visual representation of progress over time allows you to understand the progress and achievement of mokopuna, not only within their current year of learning but also across multiple years throughout their schooling.
This example shows what a mid-year report might look like for a Year 4 mokopuna. It highlights progress only against the expectations for their current year, rather than comparing them with other years. This gives a clear picture of whether they are meeting, exceeding, or still progressing toward this year’s expectations.
The tohu aromatawai describe mokopuna progress across the school year. Mokopuna who has achieved E mātau ana | proficiency is prepared for the next year of learning.
The next section on the page provides further explanation of the image displayed.

## Supporting mokopuna with complex learning needs #
The 5 components described above will appear in reports for all mokopuna in Aotearoa, including those with complex learning needs.
The Tohu Aromatawai chosen by the kaiako will show how your mokopuna is progressing toward curriculum expectations, and the written explanation will describe this progress in more detail, recognising individual needs or learning plans for your mokopuna.
If your mokopuna has complex learning needs and requires tailored support for their learning, this does not mean that they are not proficient in the curriculum. They should not be automatically assessed as ‘E maianga ana’, or ‘E whanake haere ana’.
For mokopuna with diverse learning needs, or who have individualised progress goals and assessments outlined in their Individual Education Support Plans (IEP), reporting should reflect progress toward those personalised goals, alongside curriculum expectations.
- Parents and caregivers
